result:
The rain doesn’t fall here the way it does on Earth. It arrives in slow, deliberate sheets that glow faintly blue under the three moons. I stand on the balcony of the old observatory, coat soaked through, watching the droplets cling to the glass like hesitant thoughts. 

Below, the city pulses in soft silence. No engines, no advertisements, just the low hum of living crystal that lines the streets and answers when you speak to it. People don’t shout anymore. They learned that the architecture listens.

I came here chasing a rumor: that on this planet, memory isn’t stored in the brain but in the wind. Every regret you carry is eventually lifted from your shoulders and turned into weather. That’s why the storms taste like salt and childhood. That’s why lovers stand in the downpour without umbrellas, letting the rain take what they no longer need.

Last night I finally let it have my father’s voice. The one that still scolded me in dreams. I stood in the central square at 3 a.m. and screamed every cruel thing he ever said until my throat bled. The wind answered by wrapping around me like tired arms. This morning I woke up lighter. My hands didn’t shake when I poured the tea.

Some nights I wonder if the planet is keeping the memories somewhere safe, polishing them into something kinder. Maybe one day it will rain them back down as forgiveness.

I hope I’m still here when that happens. I want to open my mouth and finally taste what peace feels like.

(Word count: 248)
